tech.headline: Solar Subsidies Drove 90% Cost Collapse Globally
tech.summary: Global solar module manufacturing received approximately $21 billion in subsidies since 2010, with China accounting for over 81%. While China dominates, OECD countries have recently increased support. These subsidies are credited with driving a 90% reduction in solar costs worldwide through industrial-scale manufacturing incentives.

tech.headline: Energy Storage Becomes Core Grid Infrastructure
tech.summary: At SNEC 2026, energy storage evolved from a PV add-on to essential grid infrastructure. Key drivers include long-duration systems, grid-forming technology, sodium-ion scaling, and AI data center demand. The industry now emphasizes system-level competition, prioritizing duration, intelligence, and full-stack capabilities over standalone battery performance.

tech.summary: JinkoSolar reached 34.82% efficiency for a perovskite-silicon tandem solar cell, validated by China's Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology. This result highlights progress in tandem cell technology for higher solar energy conversion rates.

tech.headline: Battery-Free Artificial Photosynthesis for Solar Fuel
tech.summary: Osaka Metropolitan University scientists developed a battery-free artificial photosynthesis system. The system self-regulates energy output without external electronics or batteries. It uses a solid electrolyte to adapt to sunlight fluctuations. This innovation promises cheaper and cleaner solar fuel production. The technology eliminates reliance on external power sources for energy management. The breakthrough could revolutionize sustainable energy generation and storage methods. The research highlights advancements in autonomous energy systems and renewable resource utilization.

learn.title: Duck Curve Explained
learn.intro: Solar power peaks mid‑day, but as clouds clear or temperatures drop, output plummets just before sunset. This sudden dip forces utilities to ramp up gas plants or shed load, creating the infamous duck curve. Understanding it is key to keeping the grid stable while scaling solar.
learn.c1_text: During the warmest part of the day, rooftop panels can generate up to 30% more electricity than the average household needs. Imagine a bathtub filling faster than the drain can empty—if the drain (grid demand) slows, the water (solar output) spills over.
learn.c2_text: As people return home, lights, heat pumps, and appliances turn on. The grid must supply this surge while solar drops, often by 70% in just 30 minutes, causing utilities to pull extra power from fossil plants.
learn.c3_text: India’s 10 GW of rooftop solar now contributes 15% of daytime generation. When sunset hits, that surplus disappears, stressing the national grid that already struggles with load peaks.
learn.stat2: Solar output drops 70% in 30 minutes at sunset
learn.stat3: US DOE estimates $2.5 billion annual cost of duck curve mitigation
learn.stat4: Peak demand can rise 20 MW in 15 minutes during sunset

learn.title: Demand Response Basics
learn.intro: Large industrial users can shift or shave power use when the grid signals a need. Demand response turns consumers into flexible resources, reducing expensive peaking plants, cutting emissions, and keeping the grid reliable during spikes or outages.
learn.c1_text: An industrial plant can pause a 5 MW air‑conditioning unit for 30 minutes during a grid peak, saving $2000 in electricity and lowering emissions by 10 tonnes of CO₂.
learn.c2_text: When the grid sends a price spike alert, users respond instantly. A 10% price increase can shave 3 MW of demand within minutes, easing strain on transmission lines.
learn.c3_text: India’s 100 MW coal plants can reduce peak load by 20% if industries participate in demand response, cutting potential blackouts during monsoon season.
learn.stat1: US DOE: 2,000 MW of demand response capacity nationwide
learn.stat2: India: 100 MW potential load reduction via DR
learn.stat3: DR can cut peak demand by 15% in large plants

learn.title: Perovskite Solar Cells
learn.intro: Perovskite materials are opening a new chapter in photovoltaics, achieving lab efficiencies above 26%—well beyond silicon’s 22%. Their low‑cost processing and flexibility promise cheaper, lighter solar panels for homes, cars, and large‑scale farms.
learn.c1_text: Perovskite’s crystalline lattice captures sunlight like a sponge, converting 26% of photons to electricity—5% higher than the best silicon panels, comparable to a 1 kW panel producing 260 W more power.
learn.c2_text: Unlike silicon, perovskites can be deposited from solution at temperatures below 150 °C, reducing energy use by 80% and enabling roll‑to‑roll printing on flexible substrates.
learn.c3_text: India’s 100 GW solar target could include 30 GW of perovskite PV by 2030, cutting panel cost by 30% and boosting rooftop adoption in tier‑2 cities.
learn.stat4: Projected panel cost reduction: 30% with perovskite

learn.title: PEM vs Alkaline Electrolysis
learn.intro: Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water using electricity. PEM (proton‑exchange membrane) electrolyzers offer fast response and high purity, while alkaline units are cheaper but slower. Choosing the right technology is critical for scaling hydrogen supply chains.
learn.c1_text: PEM electrolyzers can ramp up in 10 minutes, ideal for storing excess solar during the day and delivering hydrogen when demand spikes, like a quick‑start sports car.
learn.c2_text: Alkaline units operate at 80 °C, delivering 70% efficiency but need 30 minutes to reach full output—suitable for continuous base‑load production like a steady commuter train.
learn.c3_text: India plans 5 GW of green hydrogen by 2030, favoring PEM for power‑to‑gas plants near solar farms, while alkaline units will power industrial hydrogen plants in Maharashtra.

learn.title: Second‑Life EV Batteries
learn.intro: Electric vehicles reach peak capacity after 8‑10 years. Reusing their batteries for stationary storage keeps them useful, reduces waste, and supports grid stability—turning old cars into community power banks.
learn.c1_text: A second‑life battery still retains ~70% of its original capacity, enough to power a household for 4 hours or supply a microgrid during a storm, similar to a 10 kWh backup system.
learn.c2_text: Studies show second‑life packs can be 30% cheaper than new storage, cutting the payback period from 5 years to 3, accelerating adoption in rural electrification projects.
learn.c3_text: India’s EV rollout of 3 million cars by 2030 could yield 200 GWh of second‑life batteries, enough to store 5% of the nation’s daily electricity consumption.

learn.title: EV Charging Levels
learn.intro: Electric vehicles can be charged at three speeds: Level 1 (120 V), Level 2 (240 V), and DC fast (480 V). Knowing the differences helps drivers choose the right charger for home, workplace, or quick trips, ensuring efficient use of grid resources.
learn.c1_text: Using a standard outlet, a 10 kWh battery gains ~4 kWh per hour—like refilling a 20‑liter bottle in 5 hours, suitable for overnight charging.
learn.c2_text: 240 V chargers deliver 6–10 kW, topping a 60‑kWh battery in 6–10 hours—think of filling a 50‑liter bottle in 2 hours, ideal for home or office.
learn.c3_text: DC fast chargers provide 50–150 kW, adding 80 kWh in 45 minutes—equivalent to pouring 80 liters into a jug in half an hour, perfect for highway stops.
